1:
The chapter opens on a king undone by grief, and the witnesses read the scene as a study in how private sorrow becomes a public danger. Keil and Delitzsch set the frame for the whole chapter: David's 'passionate and sinful sorrow' over Absalom so mastered him that he forgot the duty binding upon a king, and Joab was obliged to recall him to it. Matthew Henry gathers the pathos and the politics together. The messengers had scarcely brought word of the victory before Joab and the army followed 'to grace the king's triumphs,' only to find him in tears — a reception they construed as displeasure at what they had done. David 'proclaimed his passion,' crying with a loud voice for a son who had sought his ruin, and Henry judges the display 'very unwise and very unworthy,' a mourning for so bad a son as Absalom that recalled, by sad contrast, Jacob's mourning for so good a son as Joseph (Genesis 37:34).

The commentators converge on a lesson about eminence and self-government. Henry draws it out: 'The people will take particular notice what their princes say and do. The more eyes we have upon us, and the greater our influence is, the more need we have to speak and act wisely and to govern our passions strictly.' David never more needed the hearts of his subjects than now, and nothing could have been more impolitic than to slight the men who had just saved him. John Gill adds the plain narrative note that the report reached Joab before David himself had seen the king, so that the general came braced for a confrontation.

The wesleyan-arminian voice of Adam Clarke lays out the movement of the whole chapter as a table of contents — David's lament, Joab's threat, the tribes' counsel to bring the king back, the pardon of Shimei, the vindication of Mephibosheth, and the affecting farewell to Barzillai — reminding the reader that this single verse is the hinge on which a long day of reckoning turns. The witnesses do not divide here; they agree that grief indulged past its season, however natural in a bereaved father, had become in a king a fault that endangered a throne only just recovered, and that the very love which made David weep for Absalom made him blind, for the moment, to the loyal thousands who wept to see him weep.
2:
The witnesses read this verse as the strange spectacle of a victorious army creeping home in shame, and they trace the reversal to a single cause: the king's grief had infected the whole people. John Gill states it simply — the people 'mourned too, instead of expressing joy upon the occasion,' their triumph swallowed up in their sovereign's sorrow. Keil and Delitzsch keep close to the Hebrew, noting how the verse flows straight into the picture of soldiers stealing into the city as men ashamed, and of the king with his face covered crying aloud, 'O my son Absalom.'

The sharpest comment belongs to the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, who fixes on the pathos of the inversion: 'Instead of rejoicing that a most unnatural and ruinous rebellion had been quashed, the people mourned over their own success, because they saw their king so immoderately afflicted for the loss of his worthless son.' The word immoderately carries the judgement all the witnesses share — that the grief was real and a father's, but had passed the bounds of what the occasion, and the throne, could bear.

Beneath the plain report lies a lesson the commentators draw about the power of leaders over the mood of those they lead. Matthew Henry, expounding this stretch of verses together, had observed that 'the report of it ran through the army' — how the king was grieved for his son — so that his private passion was published abroad and reproduced in every heart. A victory that should have been the salvation of the kingdom was 'turned into mourning,' and the men who had risked their lives were made to feel that their loyalty had wounded rather than served their master. The theological weight the witnesses leave on the verse is this: sorrow has its rights, but a shepherd's unchecked sorrow can rob a whole people of the joy that is justly theirs, and even the best of men, indulging a natural affection out of season, may 'put such hardships,' as Henry says, on those bound to honour him. The day of deliverance had become, by the king's example, a day of guilt and grief for men who had done nothing but well.
3:
This verse gives the witnesses a vivid picture — a conquering army sneaking into the city like beaten men — and they dwell on the poignancy of the reversal. John Gill paints it exactly: the people 'did not march into it in companies, in a public and triumphant manner, as conquerors used to do; but entered in a private manner, one by one, or a very few together, not caring to be seen or known, at least by the king, as fearing they had incurred his displeasure.' The victory that should have been paraded is smuggled home.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the same detail and supplies the soldiers' inward reasoning: they came 'not openly and triumphantly, as conquerors use to do; but secretly, as if they were afraid and ashamed, lest David should see them, and look upon them with an evil eye, as those that had an hand in killing of his beloved son.' The shame is not for any wrong done but for the king's misconstruction of a right thing done; the loyal are made to feel like criminals.

Both voices catch the same irony that the surrounding verses press home. The Scripture's own comparison is devastating — the men 'stole away' as an army steals away 'when they flee in battle' — so that the very bearing of defeat is stamped upon the bearing of victory. The witnesses find here a quiet indictment of David's self-absorption: a king's disordered grief has power to reverse the moral meaning of a day, turning honour into embarrassment and gratitude into fear. It is the outworking of what the previous verse announced, that the triumph was 'turned into mourning,' and it prepares the way for Joab's blunt intervention. The commentators do not multiply words on so plain a verse; they let the image speak, and its lesson lands of itself. Those who serve faithfully deserve to be met with thanks, and when they are met instead with a cold and covered face, the fault lies not with them but with the one too wrapped in his own sorrow to see them. The soldiers' furtive homecoming is the measure of how far David had, for the moment, failed the people who loved him.
4:
The witnesses read this verse as the very portrait of a mourner, and they attend both to the gesture and to the cry. John Gill explains the covered face: David 'would not see his generals, and thank them for their services: but wrapped himself in his mantle, after the manner of mourners.' The withdrawal is deliberate and total; the king shuts out the men he ought to be honouring. Adam Clarke confirms the custom — 'this was the custom of mourners' — and then turns to the threefold cry, 'O my son Absalom! O Absalom, my son, my son!'

On that repeated name Clarke offers his most characteristic note. Following Calmet, he observes that 'the frequent repetition of the name of the defunct is common in the language of lamentation,' and he reaches, as he often does, for a classical parallel, quoting Virgil's fifth Eclogue and its threefold raising of the name of Daphnis to the stars. The learned illustration is meant to naturalize David's grief — to show that the broken, circling speech of the bereaved, returning again and again to the beloved name, is the universal grammar of sorrow, not a peculiar excess of this one father.

Yet the verse sits inside a narrative that the witnesses will not let sentiment soften. Clarke points the reader back to the previous chapter (see 2 Samuel 18:33), where the same cry first broke from David at the news of Absalom's death, so that this verse is the prolonging of a grief that had already been given its hour. The commentators' restraint is itself an argument: they let the tenderness of the lament stand — a father's heart, refusing to be comforted, is a thing they will not mock — while the very placing of the verse, between the shamed soldiers of the verse before and Joab's reproach in the verse after, exposes how such tenderness, indulged upon a throne, becomes a public wound. The covered face that hides the king's tears also hides from him the faces of the loyal, and the name repeated in love is heard by the army as a name preferred above their own lives. Grief and duty, the witnesses imply, are here at war, and for the moment grief has won the field.
5:
Joab now bursts into the house, and the witnesses concentrate on the strange doubleness of the man — right in his cause, wrong in his manner. Adam Clarke frames the paradox that governs all the readings: Joab's speech 'is not only remarkable for the insolence of office, but also for good sense and firmness.' Every candid reader must grant that David acted imprudently and that a firm reproof was needed to rouse him; yet Joab 'far exceeded the bonds of that reverence which a servant owes to his master, or a subject to his prince.' Clarke's verdict on the speaker is unsparing — 'a good soldier, but in every respect a bad man, and a dangerous subject.'

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott draws the fullest portrait, and it is a chilling one. This verse, he notes, resumes the thread of the chapter's opening, the intervening verses being parenthetical; and in it 'Joab's whole character appears strikingly.' With his hand still 'red with the blood of the beloved son,' he goes 'in the hardest and most unfeeling terms to reproach the father for giving way to his grief,' treating the king 'with thorough insolence and with the air of a superior' — yet withal counselling David 'for his own welfare and for that of the kingdom as a wise and loyal statesman.' Ellicott even wonders whether David yet knew of Joab's own part in Absalom's death, which would make the reproach the more brazen.

The specific charge — that David had shamed the faces of the servants who saved his life and the lives of his sons, daughters, wives, and concubines — draws out the commentators' sense of the stakes. Ellicott observes what an Absalom triumphant would have meant: after the Oriental custom he would have put to death every possible rival, and points to the grim precedents of such dynastic slaughter (Judges 9:5; 1 Kings 16:11; 2 Kings 11:1). John Gill pictures Joab tracking David from the gate-chamber to his palace, where he 'continued the same doleful ditty as at first.' John Wesley glosses the wound Joab names: David had 'shamed' his men 'by disappointing their just hopes of praises and rewards, and by requiting them with contempt and tacit rebukes.' The witnesses thus hold two truths at once — that the reproof was true and needed, and that its bearer was a bloodstained, overbearing man who spoke it without a shred of the deference a king was owed.
6:
Joab's accusation reaches its sharpest point — 'thou lovest thine enemies, and hatest thy friends' — and the witnesses weigh it carefully, unwilling to let a half-truth pass for the whole truth. John Gill is the most exact: the charge was 'not strictly true,' though 'there was some appearance of it, which is here greatly exaggerated.' David had shown so much grief for Absalom his enemy, who had rebelled against him, and so little regard for the friends who had exposed their lives for him, that Joab's overstatement had a colour of justice even where it lacked full accuracy.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the Hebrew hyperbole in its full harshness: David loves 'those who hate thee, and hatest those who love thee,' and by his conduct has 'given to know' that commanders and soldiers 'are nothing' to him — indeed that had Absalom lived and all of them died, 'it would be right in thine eyes.' It is a cruel construction of a father's tears, pressing them to a monstrous conclusion; yet the witnesses recognize that Joab framed it so precisely because a milder word would not have pierced.

That is the point John Wesley underlines. The accusation 'is not to be understood as exactly true; but David's carriage gave too much colour to such a suggestion; and such sharpness of speech was in a manner necessary to awaken the king out of his lethargy, and to preserve him from the impendent mischiefs.' Here the commentators find the verse's abiding lesson. Blunt speech, even overstated speech, may be the instrument God uses to rouse a good man sunk in a dangerous mood; the exaggeration that would be slander in one mouth becomes, in the providence of the moment, the shock that saves. The witnesses are agreed that Joab wronged David in the manner and in the measure of his words, and that Absalom was in no true sense David's beloved friend nor the army his enemy; but they are equally agreed that the king's own excess had lent the lie its plausibility. A ruler who so publicly prefers a dead traitor to his living defenders should not be astonished if his defenders, and a rough soldier speaking for them, conclude that their lives are held cheap. The remedy Joab presses in the next verse follows from this reproach as its natural cure.
7:
Joab moves from reproach to command — 'arise, go forth, and speak comfortably unto thy servants' — backed by a solemn oath, and the witnesses ask two questions of the verse: was the counsel right, and was the threat a warning or a menace? On the counsel all agree. Keil and Delitzsch judge that Joab 'was certainly not only justified, but bound in David's own interests, to expostulate with him,' since the king's conduct 'would necessarily stifle the affection of the people towards their king, and might be followed by the most serious results with reference to his throne.' To 'speak to the heart' of the servants is a Hebrew idiom for friendly, reassuring speech, as when Joseph comforted his brethren (Genesis 50:21).

On the oath the witnesses divide over its temper. John Gill reads Joab as overstepping throughout: he 'seems to take too much upon him, and to speak unbecoming a subject, and not with that decency as was necessary in speaking to a king.' Yet Gill supplies the historical excuse — 'a general of an army had great power over princes in those times, and presumed very much on their interest in the army,' which led such men to lay aside the decorum that became them. The insolence is real, but it is the insolence of a age when the sword often overawed the crown.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the oath more charitably. The thing Joab swears 'by the Lord' is not, he insists, a threat that 'he will lead the people into revolt — he does not seem to have conceived, far less to have expressed, any such design.' It is 'simply an assurance of the extreme danger of the course David was pursuing, put in such a strong and startling way as to rouse him from the selfishness of his sorrow.' On this reading the warning that not a man will tarry with the king by night, and that this would prove worse than all the evil of his life, is prophecy rather than blackmail — a faithful forecast of what desertion would do, dressed in the roughest words to make it land. Between Gill's overbearing subject and Ellicott's blunt but loyal counsellor the witnesses leave the reader to judge; but they are one in holding that the substance of the advice was sound, and that David's throne was in real peril from nothing so much as his own grief. The verse turns on the paradox that the harshest voice in the room was, in this instance, the truest friend.
8:
David yields, and the witnesses mark the swiftness and dignity of his recovery. Keil and Delitzsch state it plainly: 'Nevertheless David was obliged to yield to his representations. The king rose up, and sat in the gate,' and all the people came before him — the troops filing past a sovereign who now, we may supply from the context, 'manifested his good-will in both looks and words.' The gesture is public and deliberate: the covered face of the mourner gives way to the open face of the king, seated where a king belongs.

The witnesses dwell on the place. John Gill notes that the gate was 'a public place, where the inhabitants met on divers accounts,' and Adam Clarke sharpens it — 'the place where justice was administered to the people.' By taking his seat there David resumes not merely his visibility but his office; he is again the judge and shepherd of Israel, no longer the man hiding in an inner room. Matthew Henry, expounding this recovery, draws the pastoral moral that the whole scene has been driving toward: 'When we are convinced of a fault, we must amend, though we are told of it by our inferiors, and indecently, or in heat and passion.' David 'shook off his grief, anointed his head, and washed his face,' and 'timely giving way usually prevents the ill effects of mistaken measures.'

The verse also opens the chapter's second theme, and the commentators note the pivot. The same sentence that seats the king reports that 'Israel had fled every man to his tent.' Charles Ellicott insists on the meaning of the word: 'Israel' here, as throughout this narrative, denotes not the loyal nation but those who had espoused Absalom's cause (compare 2 Samuel 18:17), and 'had fled' should be read 'but Israel fled' — the beaten rebels scattering home even as the king reappears. So the verse both closes the drama of David's grief and quietly begins the drama of his restoration: the throne is reclaimed, the rebel host dispersed, and the long question of how the king shall be brought back to Jerusalem is set in motion. The witnesses find in David's obedience a model of teachableness — a great man mending a real fault the moment it is shown him, and by that meekness saving what his sorrow had endangered.
9:
The scene shifts from Mahanaim to the whole land, and the witnesses read the tribes' quarrel as the first stirring of a guilty conscience toward its rightful king. Keil and Delitzsch catch the movement: with the rebellion crushed and Absalom's followers scattered home, 'there arose a movement among all the tribes in favour of David,' the people 'casting reproaches at one another' as they remembered how the king had saved them from their enemies and from the Philistines, and how they had driven such a benefactor out of the land. The strife, they judge, 'arose from the consciousness of having done an injustice to the king.'

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gives the fullest reflection, and he begins with the puzzle: why did David not simply march back and seize his capital while the rebels were in confusion? Because, Henry answers, 'he would go back as a prince, with the consent and unanimous approbation of the people, and not as a conqueror forcing his way'; he would restore their liberties rather than seize them, and go home 'not at the head of his forces, but in the arms of his subjects.' A king who can make himself his people's darling 'looks greater' than one who makes himself their terror. Henry reads the tribes' dispute charitably — perhaps it was not whether to bring the king back, on which all agreed, but 'whose fault it was that it was not done,' every man justifying himself and blaming his neighbour. He warns that 'mutual excitements to the doing of a good work are laudable, but not mutual accusations for the not doing of it.'

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds the sober note that 'with the collapse of the rebellion the accompanying infatuation passed away, and the people began to remember how much they owed to David,' though the movement was as yet 'without organisation or leadership.' John Wesley supplies the theological undertone: their fresh memory of David's high merits was itself a work of God, 'being now reconciled to David,' who 'brings afresh to their memories' the services they had forgotten. John Gill notes only that all the tribes save Judah were at this strife. The witnesses agree in reading the passage typologically as well as historically: as Henry will press in the verses following, the true King, the Son of David, will rule in those hearts that invite him and not till he is invited — first bowing the heart and making it willing, then reigning in the midst of his enemies (Psalms 110:2-3).
10:
The tribes press their argument to its point — Absalom, whom they had anointed, is dead; why then does no one speak of bringing the king back? — and the witnesses attend both to the fact of that anointing and to the delay it now rebukes. John Gill reads the anointing loosely: it 'either was really done by Absalom's party, or in effect by proclaiming and appointing him king.' The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the point, for 'there is no other mention of the anointing of Absalom,' and it 'certainly would not have been performed by the high-priests.' It may have been done by some prophet, he allows, or the word may be a mere form of speech 'taken from the custom of anointing, and only mean whom we appointed over us.'

On the delay the witnesses hear a note of danger under the hesitation. Ellicott finds an evident 'hesitation and delay, arising probably from a mere want of organisation, but yet of dangerous tendency' — and it is precisely here, he observes, that David 'shows that politic power which had so often before stood him in good stead.' Keil and Delitzsch trace the textual detail that the words of all Israel reached the king and prompted his message to the priests, noting how the Septuagint and some codices of the Vulgate repeat the clause. John Wesley supplies the human sense of the exchange: it is 'the people of Israel' speaking thus 'to the elders of Israel'; seeing their designs for Absalom disappointed, 'they now repented of that undertaking, and were willing to testify so much by their forwardness to bring back David, and re-establish him.'

The commentators do not divide sharply here; they read the verse as the pivot on which the chapter's politics turn. The very people who had anointed a usurper now stand reproaching themselves for their silence toward their true king, yet no one moves first. Their question, 'why speak ye not?', hangs unanswered until David, learning of it, takes the initiative and turns the tribes' aimless remorse into an organised recall. The witnesses find in this a study of penitence that is real but paralysed — a people convicted of their fault yet unable, of themselves, to take the first step home — and they leave the reader to see how the king's wisdom, and behind it God's providence, must supply the movement the people cannot generate on their own.
11:
David now acts, and the witnesses admire the shrewdness with which he works upon his own tribe through the two priests. Adam Clarke lays bare the policy: 'David was afraid to fall out with this tribe: they were in possession of Jerusalem, and this was a city of great importance to him.' Judah had joined Absalom and were 'doubtless now ashamed of their conduct'; David 'appears to take no notice of their infidelity, but rather to place confidence in them, that their confidence in him might be naturally excited' — and, to bind them further, to promise Amasa the command of the host in Joab's place.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott explains why Judah of all tribes was slowest. It had 'shown especial ingratitude to David, and had formed the cradle and centre of the rebellion,' and even now Jerusalem 'probably had a garrison of Absalom's soldiers.' The men of Judah 'might naturally doubt how they would be received, and their military organisation in Absalom's interest threw especial obstacles in their way.' Their hesitation, then, was not indifference but fear and entanglement, which David's message is designed to dissolve.

The commentators note the pointed reproach David sends — why should Judah be 'the last' to bring back the king, when the speech of all Israel had already reached him? John Wesley reads the reproach through Judah's guilt: they 'being the abettors of Absalom's rebellion, despaired of pardon, and therefore were backward to promote the king's restoration.' John Gill supplies the plausible detail that David sent to Zadok and Abiathar 'perhaps by Ahimaaz and Jonathan their sons,' the same young runners who had served him through the rebellion. The witnesses observe a textual curiosity too: Ellicott suggests that the closing 'to his house' 'may be an accidental repetition from the previous clause,' and Wesley takes the king's 'house' to mean Mahanaim, where the royal household now was. Beneath the diplomacy the commentators see a consistent portrait of David the statesman — governing by winning hearts rather than by force, choosing to be invited home rather than to impose himself, and turning even the tribe most deeply implicated in his overthrow into the first to welcome him back. It is the same politic gentleness the whole chapter displays: the king who could have come as a conqueror chooses to come as a brother.
12:
The heart of David's appeal to Judah is a claim of kinship — 'ye are my brethren, ye are my bones and my flesh' — and the witnesses dwell on the tenderness and the tactics folded together in the phrase. John Gill hears in it both affection and reassurance: being of the same tribe, David would 'deal gently with them, as if they were parts of his body, and not be severe upon them for the hand they had in the conspiracy, as they might fear.' The very fear that held Judah back — that their treason would be avenged — is what the claim of blood is meant to disarm.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the words themselves, noting that the Hebrew is more exactly 'bone' (as in the next verse and in 2 Samuel 5:1, where all Israel had once owned David as their bone and flesh). Of course, he grants, Judah 'was more closely connected with him by blood than any other'; but 'the point likely to influence them was that the king recognised this relationship' — that David chose, in the hour of their guilt, to appeal to the tie rather than to press the offence. Keil and Delitzsch read the verse as of a piece with the message that follows, David inclining the heart of Judah 'as of one man' to invite him home.

The commentators find here a study in how kinship acknowledged can accomplish what force could not. David does not minimize what Judah had done, nor does he threaten; he lays the claim of shared flesh across the wound and lets it heal the breach. The reproach 'wherefore then are ye the last?' is folded inside the endearment, so that the rebuke stings only enough to move, not to alienate. Several of the witnesses, following the chapter's typological grain, cannot help hearing the deeper resonance that Matthew Henry made explicit earlier: 'the Son of David has been pleased to call us brethren, his bone and his flesh, which encourages us to hope that we shall find favour with him.' The King who might justly reckon with our rebellion instead claims us as kindred, and by that claim draws the guilty home. On the plain historical level and the typological level alike, the witnesses read the verse as mercy taking the initiative, sovereignty stooping to the language of family that it may win rather than compel.
13:
David's boldest and most disputed stroke is the oath to Amasa — 'thou shalt be captain of the host before me continually in the room of Joab' — and the witnesses weigh its wisdom against its justice. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott gives the sharpest verdict. Amasa, like Joab, was David's nephew; and in offering the command-in-chief to Absalom's own general 'David adopted a bold, but a rash and unjust policy.' Amasa 'should have been punished, not rewarded for his treason'; he had given 'no evidence of loyalty,' and the appointment was 'sure to provoke the jealousy and hostility of Joab.' Yet Ellicott sees the calculation behind it: David had 'long been restless under the overbearing influence of Joab,' and since Joab had murdered Absalom against the king's express command 'was determined to be rid of him,' seizing this chance 'to win over to himself what remained of the military organisation of Absalom.'

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the move as David's release from a hated bondage. Joab 'had lately exasperated the king by his murder of Absalom, contrary to David's express command,' and so the king, 'having now the opportunity of another person who had a greater interest than Joab, gladly complies with it, that so he might both chastise Joab for his faults and rescue himself from the bondage in which Joab had hitherto held him.' John Gill keeps to Amasa's state of mind: as Absalom's general 'he might fear he should never be pardoned, whoever was, and looked upon his case as desperate,' having led the rebel army against the king's forces.

The witnesses thus hold together admiration and censure. As policy the appointment was astute — it detached the rebel soldiery from any lingering cause and freed David from a general grown 'intolerably haughty.' As justice it was indefensible: a traitor confirmed in the highest command over the loyal, and a promise sworn with God's name to a man who had earned punishment. Matthew Henry had already noted the tragic sequel, that David 'did not do kindly by Amasa in letting his design be known, for it occasioned his death by Joab's hand' in the chapter following. The commentators find in the whole transaction a characteristic mixture in David — genuine wisdom entangled with rashness, mercy that shades into imprudence — and a warning that even a shrewd stroke of state, if it tramples justice, sows the seed of later blood.
14:
The stroke succeeds — David 'bowed the heart of all the men of Judah, even as the heart of one man' — and the witnesses first settle who did the bowing. John Gill records that 'the Jewish commentators generally understand this of Amasa,' that he used his interest with Judah until they agreed as one man; 'but it seems best,' Gill judges, 'to understand it of David, who by these gentle methods, kind messages, and affectionate speeches powerfully inclined and engaged the hearts of the people.' Keil and Delitzsch agree flatly: 'the subject is David, not Amasa or Zadok.'

What the witnesses most admire is the manner of the return the verse secures. Gill draws it out: David 'could have come without their leave, or any invitation from them, as he was their lawful king, and a victorious one, the rebellion being crushed'; he had the power 'to chastise those concerned in it, and use them with severity.' But 'he chose rather to gain the hearts of his people, and to come in a way peaceable to them and honourable to himself.' The unanimity — the whole tribe moved as one man — is the fruit of gentleness, not of force. Adam Clarke distils the statecraft into a maxim: the measures David used 'were the best calculated' to this end, for 'appear to distrust those whom you have some reason to suspect, and you increase their caution and distrust; put as much confidence in them as you safely can, and this will not fail to excite their confidence towards you.'

Yet the witnesses do not let the success blind them to its flaw. Keil and Delitzsch commend the wisdom of appealing to Judah, lest it seem the king's own tribe were 'dissatisfied with his victory' and the old jealousy between Judah and Israel be inflamed; 'but it was not only unwise, but unjust,' they add, 'to give to Amasa, the traitor-general of the rebels, a promise on oath' of the command. So the verse stands as both a triumph of the reconciling art and a monument to its cost. The commentators read the bowing of Judah's heart as a picture of how love wins what compulsion cannot — the king restored by invitation, not by conquest — while the means by which one heart was bowed, the rash oath to Amasa, waits in the wings to trouble the peace it purchased. Gentleness gathers the people; imprudence, meanwhile, arms the next quarrel.
15:
The king turns homeward and reaches the Jordan, and the witnesses mark the meeting of the two parties at the river as the threshold of the restoration. John Gill supplies the itinerary with his usual precision, noting from Bunting that the distance from Mahanaim to the Jordan was some twenty-eight miles. Keil and Delitzsch lay out the choreography: when David reached the river on his return, 'Judah had come to Gilgal to meet him, to conduct the king over the Jordan' — that is, to form an escort at the crossing — and it is at this moment that Shimei the Benjamite comes hurrying down from Bahurim (see 2 Samuel 16:5) with the men of Judah to meet the king.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fixes the geography exactly: 'the two parties met at the Jordan, David coming from Mahanaim to the eastern side of the ford, near Jericho, and the representatives of the tribe of Judah to Gilgal on the opposite bank.' The picture is of a king waiting on the far shore while his own tribe gathers on the near one, the river itself standing between exile and homecoming, to be crossed only when the people have come out to bring him over.

The witnesses treat this verse as a hinge — the last line of the negotiation and the first of the reunion — and several of them cannot pass the Jordan without recalling its weight in David's story and in Israel's. It was 'from the land of Jordan' that David, in his flight, had remembered God (Psalms 42:6), and it was this same river that Israel had first crossed under Joshua into the land of promise. Now the crossing is reversed and redeemed: the anointed king, driven out over its waters, is brought back across them by a people returning to their allegiance. The commentators do not multiply comment on so transitional a verse; they let the setting carry the meaning. The restoration is not seized but received; the king comes as far as the river and there waits to be met, and the meeting at Gilgal — the place of Israel's first memorial in the land — becomes the stage on which the pardons and reconciliations of the following verses will be played out. The homeward journey has reached its decisive water, and all that remains is to be carried over.
16:
At the Jordan the first of the chapter's suppliants appears, and the witnesses read Shimei's haste as the calculation of a man who knows which way the wind has turned. Charles Ellicott is blunt about his motives: Shimei 'was a man of influence and importance, and his accession to David at this juncture was of great value'; yet 'it is plain that Shimei himself was only a time-server, and that he was thoroughly disloyal in his heart, and only came now to David because he saw that his was the winning cause.' Adam Clarke confirms the man's standing from his retinue — 'it appears that Shimei was a powerful chieftain in the land, for he had here, in his retinue, no less than a thousand men.'

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gives the passage its typological grandeur. 'Perhaps Jordan was never passed with so much solemnity, nor with so many remarkable occurrences, as it was now, since Israel passed it under Joshua.' Two men meet David on its banks, both of whom 'had abused him wretchedly when he was in his flight' — Ziba, who had wronged him 'with his fair tongue,' and Shimei, who had wronged him 'with his foul tongue,' railing and cursing (see 2 Samuel 16:5). Had David been defeated, Henry observes, Shimei 'would have continued to trample upon him'; but seeing him come home in triumph, 'he thinks it his interest to make his peace.' From this Henry draws his solemn application: 'Those who now slight and abuse the Son of David would be glad to make their peace too when he shall come in his glory; but it will be too late.' Shimei's wisdom was to 'agree with his adversary quickly, while he was in the way.'

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch attend to the crossing's logistics — Shimei with his thousand Benjamites and Ziba with his household 'went over the Jordan before the king,' through a ford, while the ferry-boat plied to carry over the royal family — and they settle a knot in the Hebrew, that Shimei 'fell down before the king' when David 'was about to cross over,' not after Shimei himself had crossed. The witnesses thus hold the scene at two levels: historically, a self-interested traitor rushing to secure himself before his winning king; typologically, a picture of hasty repentance that runs to make its peace while peace can still be made — a warning to every reader to seek the Son of David's favour now, in the day of grace, before the day of glory closes the door.
17:
The witnesses read the thousand men of Benjamin who came with Shimei as at once a display of his power and a hedge against his danger. John Gill canvasses who they were: either 'a band of soldiers, of which he was the chiliarch,' or 'tenants of his, which showed him to be a great man,' or 'his neighbours, and persons of some figure, whom he prevailed upon to come as intercessors for him.' Any of the three serves Shimei's purpose — a crowd at his back to give weight to his suit and witnesses to his submission.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the motive most fully. Shimei brought his thousand 'partly to show his interest in the people, and partly as intercessors on his behalf, and as witnesses of David's clemency or severity, that in him they might see what the rest of them might expect.' The calculation is shrewd: by making his case public he makes his pardon, if granted, a precedent that will draw the other doubtful Benjamites in. Of Ziba, who came in the same company, Wesley notes the like self-interest — 'being conscious of his former abuse of David, and of his master Mephibosheth, which he knew the king would understand,' Ziba 'designed to sweeten David's spirit towards him by forwardness in meeting him.'

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the phrase 'before the king,' which is literally 'before the face of' the king and means simply that 'they went over Jordan to meet the king.' He catches the eagerness in the picture: 'in their eagerness to prove their very doubtful allegiance, they dashed through the waters of the ford, and met the king on the eastern side of the Jordan.' The men who had lately stood with the rebellion now plunge through the river to reach their sovereign first. The witnesses find in the scene a study of doubtful loyalty overcompensating — a suspect faction rushing to be conspicuous in its devotion — and they let the reader weigh how much of such zeal is repentance and how much is fear. Shimei's thousand are both his honour guard and his insurance; the crowd that proves his standing is also the audience before whom he means to buy his life. Yet the commentators do not scorn the movement outright, for it is the shape penitence often takes among the great — public, hedged, self-protective — and David, as the following verses show, will meet even this calculated homage with a mercy larger than it deserved.
18:
The ferry-boat that crosses to carry over the king's household draws the witnesses first to a small textual curiosity and then to the drama that unfolds beside it. John Gill reports the range of opinion about the craft: it served to bring over 'his wives and children, who could not so well ford the river on foot,' and 'some will have this to be a bridge of boats, a pontoon,' while 'Abarbinel thinks it might be a company of men who carried the women and children on their shoulders, one after another.' Adam Clarke notes that this is 'the first mention of any thing of the kind,' and that 'some think a bridge or raft is what is here intended.'

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott corrects the tense to fix the geography: it should read 'as he was coming over,' or 'as he was about to cross,' for 'Shimei and Ziba met the king on the east of Jordan, and his crossing is not spoken of until' the later verses that describe him going over with Barzillai. The meeting, then, happens on the exile side of the river, before the king has set foot again in his own land — Shimei throwing himself down while David still stands on the eastern bank.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the verse straight into Shimei's plea, which it introduces: the ferry-boat 'went over to carry over the king's household, and to do what he thought good' — that is, to be placed at the king's sole disposal — 'and Shimei the son of Gera fell down before the king' as he was about to cross. On the Hebrew phrasing they insist, against the alternative, that it means David's crossing and not Shimei's. The witnesses thus turn a verse about a boat into the frame for the encounter it stages. The homely detail — a special vessel sent for the royal family while the troops shift for themselves at the ford — quietly signals that the king is being received with honour, borne back over the water that had carried him out; and against that backdrop of restored dignity the trembling suppliant falls at his feet, so that mercy and majesty meet at the river's edge. The commentators leave the theological weight where the narrator sets it: everything is 'to do what he thought good,' the whole scene now waiting upon the king's pleasure.
19:
Shimei's petition is a model of the penitent's language, and the witnesses read every clause of it. John Gill fixes on the heart of the plea — 'let not my lord the king impute iniquity unto me' — and explains its force: the request is not that the sin be denied but that it be forgiven, 'for non-imputation of sin is in effect the pardon of it.' To ask that iniquity not be reckoned to one's account is to ask, in the deepest biblical sense, for grace; and Shimei, whatever his motives, has stumbled upon the very grammar of mercy.

The witnesses set the plea within the fuller portrait of his submission that Matthew Henry had drawn. Shimei 'fell down before the king, as a penitent, as a supplicant; and, that he might be thought sincere, he did it publicly before all David's servants, and his friends the men of Judah, yea, and before his own thousand.' The reasoning Henry supplies is sound: 'the offence was public, therefore the submission ought to be so.' Here Shimei owns his crime, and in the next verse will aggravate it — 'thy servant doth know that I have sinned; I did perversely' — begging the king to deal with him not as he deserved. The word Shimei chooses, that David not 'take it to his heart,' is the plea that the king would not treasure up the wrong for vengeance.

The commentators are alive to the ambiguity of such eloquence. Shimei's words are exactly right; whether his heart is right the sequel will test, and later Scripture will show it wanting. Yet the witnesses will not for that reason despise the form of penitence he offers, for it is the appointed way a wrongdoer must come — open confession, no excuse, no claim of right, the naked appeal to be spared. On the plain level of the history the verse shows a shrewd man saving his neck by the correct words; on the level the commentators cannot help drawing, it shows what every sinner must say who would come to the King — that he has done perversely, that he asks no reckoning of his account, that he casts himself wholly on a mercy he has not earned. The very phrases Shimei uses to buy his life are the phrases in which real repentance clothes itself, and David, in the verses following, will answer them with a clemency that outruns the suppliant's sincerity.
20:
Shimei presses his plea with a confession and a claim of priority, and the witnesses weigh both. John Gill hears in 'thy servant doth know that I have sinned' the marks of a true acknowledgement: Shimei 'was sensible of it, and sorry for it, and publicly acknowledged it before all the men he brought with him, and before all the servants of David,' offering this early submission 'as a token of the sincerity of his repentance, and as an earnest of his future fidelity.' Adam Clarke is more guarded — the confession 'was all he could do; his subsequent conduct alone could prove his sincerity' — and yet grants that 'on such an avowal as this David could not but grant him his life.'

The claim that he came 'the first of all the house of Joseph' sets the grammatical-historical commentators to work on a phrase that seems, at first, misapplied. Charles Ellicott observes that Shimei 'was not strictly of the house of Joseph, but of Benjamin,' and concludes that 'Joseph, as the name of the most prominent member, stands for all the tribes outside of Judah.' He notes that some have made the usage an argument for a late date of the book, but answers that it is found early and widely (compare 1 Chronicles 5:1-2; Psalms 80:1-2), 'and there is no reason why the expression may not have been used at the earliest date' when Judah and the other tribes began to be distinguished. John Wesley supplies the reason Benjamin could be reckoned under Joseph: the rights of the firstborn were divided between Judah and Joseph (1 Chronicles 5:2), and before the kingdoms divided Benjamin 'marched under the standard of the house of Joseph, or of Ephraim' (Numbers 10:22-24).

The witnesses read Shimei's boast of being first with a knowing eye. To be foremost in submission was to be foremost in claim upon the king's clemency; Shimei means his early return to earn a mercy the latecomers may not receive, and, as Matthew Henry observed, 'he came first, that by his example the rest might be induced, and by his experience of the king's clemency the rest might be encouraged to follow.' The commentators thus hold together the man's genuine confession and his calculated timing. His words own the sin without excuse — which is right; his haste to be first turns even repentance to advantage — which is human. David, they show, will grant the life that such a confession asks, leaving the truth of the heart to a future that later proved it hollow.
21:
Abishai's fierce interjection — 'shall not Shimei be put to death, because he cursed the Lord's anointed?' — draws the witnesses to the man and his zeal. John Gill identifies him at once as 'the same person that would have taken off the head of Shimei at the time he cursed David, if he would have given him leave' (see 2 Samuel 16:9). Abishai's temper is consistent: what he wished to do in the day of David's flight he now, with the law restored, presses to have done in the day of David's return.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the verse as opening the exchange that runs through David's refusal and oath, and set the whole cluster of Shimei's pardon against the darker sequel of Mephibosheth, which they judge admits 'still less of justification.' But it is Matthew Henry who probes the motive. Abishai's demand had a colour of justice — Shimei had indeed cursed the Lord's anointed, and now that David's 'judicial power was restored, why should not the law have its course?' Yet Henry finds the counsel wrong at root: 'Abishai herein consulted what he supposed to be David's feelings more than his true interest.' It is the flattering severity that presumes to serve a prince by gratifying his supposed resentment, and Henry draws the warning: 'Princes have need to arm themselves against temptations to severity.'

The witnesses thus set two spirits side by side — the suppliant confessing at the king's feet and the soldier demanding his blood — and they leave no doubt which the day of grace requires. Abishai's zeal is not for God's honour so much as for vengeance dressed as justice; the very correctness of his legal point makes it the more dangerous, for it offers the king a righteous-sounding pretext to do the cruel thing. The commentators recognize that a real offence lay on Shimei, that the curse against the anointed was no light matter, and that on the strict letter Abishai had law on his side. But they read David's coming refusal as the truer wisdom and the truer mercy, and Abishai's motion as the perennial temptation of power in the hour of its triumph — to sanctify revenge by calling it the execution of justice. The verse poses the question the next verse answers: whether a restored king shall inaugurate his return with an act of blood or an act of pardon.
22:
David's reply — 'what have I to do with you, ye sons of Zeruiah, that ye should this day be adversaries unto me?' — turns the witnesses to the striking word he uses and the wisdom behind his refusal. Charles Ellicott notes tersely that 'the word in the original here is Satan' — David calls his own zealous kinsmen his adversaries, using the very name of the accuser. Matthew Henry presses the point home: 'Those who advise us to what is wrong are really Satans, adversaries to us.' Abishai and his brother, for all their pretended zeal for the king's honour, were in this moment standing against David's own inclination, his joy, and his interest.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley unfolds both halves of David's rebuke. To be 'adversaries' means 'that you put me upon things unfit for me to do, and contrary to my interest,' for it was David's policy now 'to appease the people, and reconcile them to him, and not to give them any new distaste by acts of severity,' lest others fear he watched for occasions of revenge. And the second clause — 'do not I know that I am this day king over Israel?' — Wesley expands into a meditation of the king's own heart: 'Is not my kingdom, which was in a manner wholly lost, just now restored and assured to me? And when God hath been so merciful to me in forgiving my sin, shall I show myself revengeful to Shimei?' John Gill simply refers the reader back to the earlier scene of Shimei's cursing, where the same restraint had first appeared.

The witnesses gather the verse into a rich lesson on the temper of restored power. Henry lays out David's reasons in a row: his honour inclined him to forgive, for 'it is the glory of kings to forgive those that humble and surrender themselves'; his joy inclined him to forgive, for 'joyful days should be forgiving days'; and above all his own experience of mercy inclined him, for 'those that are forgiven must forgive.' Having just received back a kingdom he had forfeited by his sin, David will not open his reign again with blood. The commentators find here the very grammar of grace — that the pardoned man becomes a pardoning man, that the day of one's own deliverance is the fittest day to deliver others, and that the counsel of revenge, however loyal it sounds, is the counsel of an adversary. David's kingship shows itself not in the power to kill but in the freedom to forgive.
23:
David's oath — 'thou shalt not die' — closes Shimei's suit, and the witnesses read it as clemency that was also, in the event, imprudence. John Gill is careful about its limits: the king promised only that Shimei should not die 'this day by my hands, or order, or by the sword.' Matthew Henry reads the pardon as reserved and conditional, 'signed and sealed with an oath, yet being bound, no doubt, to his good behaviour, and liable to be prosecuted if he afterwards misbehaved'; so Shimei was 'reserved to be, in due time, as much a monument of the justice of the government as he was now of its clemency, and in both of its prudence.'

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott gives the fullest and most sober reading, tracing the oath to its long consequence. It 'brings to mind David's dying charge to Solomon concerning him' (1 Kings 2:8-9), 'His hoar head bring thou down to the grave with blood.' The whole transaction, Ellicott insists, is to be 'viewed from a political point.' Shimei had committed high treason in David's distress, had confessed at his return and helped his restoration, and had accordingly been pardoned; but David, 'somewhat rashly, had confirmed this pardon with an oath, in such a way that he was unable to punish any subsequent treasonable tendencies.' From Shimei's character, and from Solomon's later dealing with him, 'it is plain that he remained thoroughly disloyal.' David 'saw this, and hindered by his oath from treating him as he deserved, pointed out the case to Solomon,' who bound Shimei to Jerusalem and, when he violated the condition, executed him. Ellicott's verdict is exact: 'David had made a rash oath, and observed it to the letter, but no farther, towards a thorough traitor.'

The witnesses thus hold two things together without contradiction. The mercy of the moment was right and kingly — an act of that clemency which becomes a day of triumph and which no reader is asked to regret. Yet the oath that secured it was rashly sworn, tying the king's hands against a man his own judgement distrusted, and bequeathing to his successor the unfinished business of a justice deferred. The commentators find in it the recurring shape of David's virtues and faults intertwined: a generous heart outrunning prudence, a pardon so absolute that it left no room for the later reckoning the traitor's heart would require. Grace, they observe, may be genuine and still be imprudent; and an oath, once sworn, must be kept to the letter even when the wisdom of it has failed.
24:
Mephibosheth comes down to meet the king, unwashed and unkempt, and the witnesses read his neglected person as the silent refutation of the slander told against him. John Gill settles the identification first — he is called 'the son of Saul' though truly his grandson, 'and grandsons are sometimes called sons' — and John Wesley agrees, adding that his untrimmed state showed him 'a true and obstinate mourner, and evidences of the falsehood of Ziba's relation concerning him.' The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch catalogue the signs precisely: he 'had not made his feet and his beard,' nor washed his clothes, from the day the king departed until he came again in peace — 'all of them signs of deep mourning' (compare Ezekiel 24:17; the dressing of feet and beard as in Deuteronomy 21:12).

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes the day itself the theme: 'the day of David's return was a day of bringing to remembrance, a day of account, in which what had passed in his flight was called over again.' Mephibosheth's unbroken mourning through the king's exile was 'a proof of the sincerity of his joy in the king's return.' While one of the greatest glories of Israel had departed, this lame prince 'wholly neglected himself, as one abandoned to grief for the king's affliction and the kingdom's misery.' From it Henry draws the seasonable lesson: 'In times of public calamity we ought to abridge our enjoyments in the delights of sense, in conformity to the season. There are times when God calls to weeping and mourning, and we must comply with the call.'

The commentators agree that this bodily testimony was worth more than any protest of words. Adam Clarke reads it as 'the fullest proof of his sincere attachment to David and his cause,' by which 'he amply refuted the calumnies of his servant Ziba.' Charles Ellicott supplies the topographical detail that Mephibosheth 'came down from the high land of Jerusalem to meet the king in the Jordan valley,' and the philological one that 'the word for beard is used only for the moustache,' the most conspicuous mark of a mourner left untended. The witnesses thus set the scene for the interview to follow: a man whose very body has borne witness to his loyalty stands before a king who had believed a lie about him. The unshorn beard, the unwashed feet, the soiled garments are Mephibosheth's brief; before he opens his mouth, his months of self-neglect have already begun to clear his name. The commentators find in him a quiet emblem of true devotion — a grief that keeps faith in the beloved's absence, needing no witness but itself.
25:
The king's question to Mephibosheth — 'wherefore wentest not thou with me?' — sets the witnesses to reading David's tone, and they hear in its very gentleness a mind already half-persuaded of the slander's falsehood. Charles Ellicott puts it plainly: David 'had heard and believed the story of Mephibosheth's ingratitude and treachery' told by Ziba during the flight (2 Samuel 16:3-4), 'and his present remonstrance is so gentle and kindly as to show that Mephibosheth's appearance at once produced an impression, and suggested in David's mind a doubt of the truth of what Ziba had told him.' The unshorn mourner standing before him was answer enough; the accusation began to crumble at the sight.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch devote their comment to a knot in the Hebrew of the surrounding clause — whether the text means that Mephibosheth came to Jerusalem or that 'Jerusalem,' that is the inhabitants of the capital, came to meet the king. They reject the emendations proposed by others and the rendering that would make Mephibosheth the subject, holding that the sense is 'when Jerusalem came to meet the king,' with Mephibosheth among them. It is the grammarian's care to fix the scene exactly: the meeting takes place as the whole city streams out to receive its returning sovereign.

The witnesses read the verse as the opening of a courtroom of the heart. John Gill and John Wesley both suppose that Mephibosheth had 'continued near Jerusalem,' unable to go and meet the king as others did because of his lameness, and had now come at the first opportunity. That physical helplessness is quietly central to the whole scene, for it is the hinge on which Ziba's lie had turned. David's inquiry is not an accusation but an invitation to explain, and the commentators find in its mildness a picture of the good judge who, confronted with the accused in person, allows the truth to reassert itself against the report he had once too hastily credited. The king who in his flight had believed a servant's tale against his friend now, in his return, gives the friend leave to speak — and the tenderness of the question already signals which way the verdict will fall. The verse thus turns the reader from the drama of restoration to the quieter drama of a wrong being slowly undone.
26:
Mephibosheth's defence begins with a plain charge — 'my servant deceived me' — and the witnesses reconstruct the deceit from the earlier narrative. Charles Ellicott pieces it together: 'it now appears that the two asses laden with provisions which Ziba had brought to David in his flight' (2 Samuel 16:1-2) 'were those which he had been ordered to prepare for his master. When Ziba had stolen away with these, Mephibosheth was left helpless in his lameness.' The very beasts on which the lame prince meant to ride out to the king had been taken by the servant and used to curry the servant's own favour, while the master was stranded and then slandered.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch defend the plain sense of the Hebrew against those who would emend it. Mephibosheth says, in effect, 'I will have the ass saddled and go to the king; for thy servant is lame' — and there is no need, they argue, to read it as a command to a servant, since a thing may be 'said to be done by a person even though not done with his own hands,' as when Abraham 'saddled his ass' in Genesis 22:3. The lameness is the pivot of the plea: a man who could not saddle or mount for himself was wholly at the mercy of the servant who betrayed him.

The witnesses read the verse as the unmasking of a calculated treachery. John Gill and John Wesley agree in the substance — Ziba, ordered to make ready an ass, instead 'went off with that and another himself,' carrying away the means of his master's loyalty and turning it to his own advantage with the king. The commentators do not miss the cruelty of exploiting an infirmity: Ziba's whole scheme depended on Mephibosheth's helplessness, first to strand him, then to blacken him in his forced absence. The plainness of Mephibosheth's account — no elaborate self-justification, simply the fact of the deceit and the fact of his lameness — the witnesses take as itself a mark of truth. Set beside Ziba's earlier smoothness, the lame man's blunt 'my servant deceived me' carries the ring of the wronged, and prepares the way for the confession of trust and submission that follows. The verse quietly reverses the roles Ziba had assigned: the accused master proves the injured party, and the trusted servant the traitor.
27:
Mephibosheth names the wrong done him and then, remarkably, leaves the judgement wholly to the king — 'he hath slandered thy servant, but my lord the king is as an angel of God; do therefore what is good in thine eyes.' The witnesses dwell on both the accusation and the astonishing meekness that follows it. John Gill spells out the slander: Ziba had suggested that Mephibosheth 'stayed at Jerusalem with a view to the kingdom, hoping that the quarrel between David and Absalom would issue in the restoration of it to his father's family' — a calumny with 'no foundation,' since he had entertained no such thought.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch observe that Mephibosheth had not merely guessed at the slander from David's manner but had come to know it, for Ziba would not long delay to enforce against his master the grant David had made him. On the title 'as the angel of God' they gloss: the king 'sees all just as it really is' (compare 2 Samuel 14:17). John Wesley reads it the same way — David is 'as an angel' to 'distinguish between true reports and calumnies' (see 2 Samuel 14:20).

It is the temper of Mephibosheth's appeal that most strikes the witnesses. Adam Clarke paraphrases the heart of it: 'I state my case plainly and without guile; thou art too wise not to penetrate the motives from which both myself and servant have acted. I shall make no appeal; with whatsoever thou determinest I shall rest contented.' There is no clamour for justice, no demand that Ziba be punished, no insistence on his rights — only a plain telling of the truth and a total resignation to the king's discernment. The commentators find in this the mark of a man whose heart is set not on his estate but on his sovereign's favour, and who trusts the king's wisdom more than he trusts his own cause. Where Shimei had come pleading for his life and Ziba scheming for his gain, Mephibosheth comes simply telling the truth and committing all to the king. The witnesses read his likening of David to 'an angel of God' not as flattery but as faith — confidence that the returning king carries something of God's own power to see through a lie to the truth beneath, and content, whatever the outcome, to leave the matter there.
28:
Mephibosheth's argument reaches its lowly climax: his whole house were but 'dead men' before the king, yet David had set him among those who ate at the royal table — what right, then, has he to cry any more? The witnesses unfold both the confession of unworthiness and the gratitude built upon it. John Gill weighs the phrase 'men of death': it may mean they were 'worthy of death,' the house of Saul tainted by Ishbosheth's attempt on the kingdom, or, more simply, that 'their lives lay at his mercy,' liable to the fate that princes commonly dealt to a predecessor's family, whom they would 'put to death' to secure the throne. John Wesley takes the plainer sense: 'before thy tribunal, we were all at thy mercy — not my estate only, but my life also was in thy power, if thou hadst dealt with rigour, and as earthly kings use to do with their predecessors' and enemies' children.'

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the verse straight through into the king's reply, hearing in Mephibosheth's words the settled logic of a man who has already received more than he could ask: he had been given a seat at the king's own table (compare 2 Samuel 9:7, 2 Samuel 9:11), and so 'what right have I yet to cry any more unto the king?'

The witnesses find in this the crown of Mephibosheth's whole bearing. The man makes no case for his estate; he pleads instead the memory of a mercy that had already lifted him from death to the table of a king, and reckons that such kindness has forfeited all his standing to complain. Matthew Henry, expounding the interview, drew the theological parallel that lies just beneath the surface: 'We were all as dead men before God; yet he has not only spared us, but taken us to sit at his table. How little reason then have we to complain of any trouble we are in, and how much reason to take all well that God does!' The commentators read Mephibosheth as the very model of the pardoned soul — one who, remembering that he was a dead man raised to feast with the king, cannot find it in himself to murmur over a lost inheritance. His gratitude has swallowed his grievance, and the loss of an estate weighs nothing against the wonder of a life spared and a seat at the table freely given.
29:
David's abrupt ruling — 'thou and Ziba divide the land' — draws from the witnesses a rare note of open criticism, for they judge the king's justice to have failed here. Charles Ellicott is the sharpest: when Ziba first brought his false report, David 'had instantly transferred to him all his master's possessions' (2 Samuel 16:4); now, seeing the injustice, 'he ought at least to have reversed it, if not to have punished Ziba besides.' Instead, whether from lingering doubt or, 'more probably, because he was unwilling for political reasons to offend Ziba, he resorts to that halfway and compromise course which was both weak and unjust.' Yet Ellicott grants that all the circumstances — 'the continued mourning of Mephibosheth, the silence of Ziba' — concur to show the truth of the master's story.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the division more mildly, as a return to the original arrangement. At first David had given Saul's land to Mephibosheth, with Ziba and his sons to farm it and render the lord half the produce; deceived by Ziba, he had rashly granted him the whole; now 'he finds that he has acted too rashly, and therefore confirms the former grant,' merely 'placing things in statu quo and utterly annulling the gift he had made to Ziba.' Why leave the treacherous servant anything at all? Clarke answers that Ziba 'was one of the domestics of Saul,' whom David wished to favour, and had supplied the king in his distress — 'though it was certainly too great a compensation for his services.' John Wesley reads it the same way, as a restoration of the first order (2 Samuel 9:10), Ziba and his sons managing the land and paying its profits to Mephibosheth.

The witnesses thus divide over how to name the ruling — a weak and unjust compromise, or a just reinstatement of the original settlement — but they converge on a shared unease. Matthew Henry voices it: David 'either feared Ziba too much, or loved him too well, to do justice upon him' according to the law against false witness (Deuteronomy 19:18-19); 'he was now in the humour of forgiving, and resolved to make everybody easy.' The commentators find in the episode a David whose very disposition to clemency, so admirable toward Shimei, here shades into a failure of justice, leaving a proven slanderer unpunished and a loyal man only half-righted. Mercy unmoored from justice, they quietly warn, becomes its own kind of wrong.
30:
Mephibosheth's answer to the king's ruling is the most beautiful stroke in his whole story — 'yea, let him take all, forasmuch as my lord the king is come again in peace unto his own house' — and the witnesses read it as loyalty perfected. John Gill states the plain sense: Mephibosheth 'was content that that last grant should stand,' willing to let Ziba keep the whole estate. The man who had just been half-stripped of his inheritance does not clutch after even the half restored to him; the king's safe return outweighs it all.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes this verse the summit of the interview. Mephibosheth 'drowns all his care about his estate in his joy for the king's return': 'Yea, let him take all; the presence and favour of the king shall be to me instead of all.' From it Henry draws the abiding portrait of the contented saint: 'A good man can contentedly bear his own private losses and disappointments, while he sees Israel in peace, and the throne of the Son of David exalted and established. Let Ziba take all, so that David may be in peace.'

The witnesses set this generosity in deliberate contrast to the whole crowd of self-seekers that has thronged the chapter. Shimei had come bargaining for his life, Ziba scheming for another man's land, the tribes disputing over precedence and honour; Mephibosheth alone asks nothing and surrenders everything, finding his whole reward in his sovereign's welfare. The commentators read his indifference to the estate not as carelessness but as the fruit of a love rightly ordered — a heart so fixed on the person of the king that the loss of property cannot trouble it. The theological resonance the witnesses hear is unmistakable and, in Henry, explicit: the believer whose treasure is in the King himself, and in the peace and exaltation of the throne of the Son of David, can watch his earthly portion go to a Ziba without a pang, saying with Mephibosheth, let him take all, so long as the King is come again in peace. In a chapter crowded with men calculating their advantage, Mephibosheth stands as the one witness to a devotion that seeks the King and not his gifts, and the commentators leave him there as the pattern of a soul at rest in its Lord.
31:
The last and worthiest of David's returning subjects is Barzillai the Gileadite, and the witnesses greet him as the noblest figure in the chapter. John Gill sets the scene simply: Barzillai 'came down from Rogelim,' the place of his habitation, to the Jordan. Matthew Henry frames the whole episode as the counterpart to the pardons that preceded it: David had 'graced the triumphs of his restoration with the generous remission of the injuries done to him'; now he graces them 'with a no less generous reward of the kindnesses that had been shown him.'

Henry draws Barzillai's character at length. He was the man 'who, of all the nobility and gentry of that country, had been most kind to David in his distress' — a very great man with 'a large estate,' but, better, 'a large heart to do good with it.' Had Absalom prevailed, Barzillai 'would have suffered for his loyalty,' yet he had never counted the cost. Now, 'to show that he was not weary of David, though he was so great a charge to him,' the aged noble 'attended him to Jordan, and went over with him.' From this Henry draws the lesson of ordered honour: 'Let subjects learn hence to render tribute to whom tribute is due, and honour to whom honour' (Romans 13:7). Barzillai's generosity in David's exile is the pattern of what the commentators call true greatness: 'to reduced greatness generosity obliges us, and to oppressed goodness piety obliges us, to be in a particular manner kind, to the utmost of our power.'

The witnesses read the meeting at the river as a study in mutual honour. David had come as far as the Jordan waiting to be met; Barzillai comes down to conduct him over, not from any hope of gain but from a loyalty that had proved itself in the days of danger. The commentators are struck that a man of fourscore years, who had risked all for the king when the king's cause looked lost, should now take the trouble to escort him across the river with nothing to ask for himself. It is loyalty without calculation — the opposite of Shimei's and Ziba's — and it prepares the reader for the exchange that follows, in which David will press his gratitude and Barzillai, with the modesty of true worth, will decline the reward. The witnesses hold him up as the chapter's brightest example: a faithful friend in adversity, seeking no recompense, content to have served his king.
32:
The narrator pauses to tell us Barzillai's age and wealth, and the witnesses read both details as the measure of his kindness. John Gill notes that fourscore years 'was ten years beyond the common term of man's life, and reckoned a very great age in David's time.' Keil and Delitzsch explain the ground of David's offer: Barzillai 'had supplied the king with provisions during his stay in Mahanaim, because he was very wealthy,' and so David 'would gladly have taken him to Jerusalem, to repay him there for his kindness.' They render Barzillai's coming answer in full — 'how many days are there of the years of my life, that I should go up with the king to Jerusalem? I am now eighty years old.'

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to a fine point of language. The phrase 'provided the king of sustenance' uses 'an old use of the preposition of, meaning with,' and the word is the same as that translated 'feed thee' in the next verse — a word rendered 'nourish' and 'sustain' elsewhere (compare Genesis 45:11; 1 Kings 17:9). Ellicott catches the fitness the English loses: 'the king proposes to return Barzillai's service in kind, but multiplied manifold' — Barzillai had sustained David in his need, and David would now sustain Barzillai at his own table.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke gathers the character and the offer together. Barzillai 'had given full proof of his attachment to David by the supplies he had given him when he lay at Mahanaim, where his case was all but desperate; the sincerity of his congratulations now none can suspect.' And David's proposal 'was at once noble and liberal: he wished to compensate such a man, and he wished to have at hand such a friend.' The witnesses find in the verse the meeting of two generosities — the subject who had fed the king when the king had nothing, and the king who now longs to feed the subject in return. That Barzillai had helped David 'where his case was all but desperate,' at the very moment when helping him was most dangerous, is what the commentators most admire; loyalty is proved not in the day of triumph but in the day of distress. The stage is thus set for Barzillai's gracious refusal, in which the aged man will decline the honour he has so richly earned, and the mutual courtesy of king and subject will reach its most affecting height.
33:
David's invitation to Barzillai — 'come thou over with me, and I will feed thee with me in Jerusalem' — the witnesses read as gratitude seeking a way to repay a debt it feels it can never discharge. John Gill catches the reach of the offer: Barzillai had come only 'with an intent to accompany the king over Jordan; but the king meant not only to go over Jordan, but when over to go further with him, even to Jerusalem.' David would carry his benefactor all the way home to the royal city and keep him at his own table.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the verse straight into the aged man's reply, laying out the whole exchange: 'How long have I to live, that I should go up with the king unto Jerusalem? I am this day fourscore years old.' The invitation and its refusal belong together, the one measuring the king's generosity and the other the subject's modesty.

The witnesses find in David's word 'I will feed thee' the deliberate echo of Barzillai's own kindness. Matthew Henry, expounding the interview, notes the two motives folded into the offer: David invited him 'that he might have the pleasure of his company and the benefit of his counsel,' for Barzillai was 'wise and good as well as rich,' and 'it is what a man is, more than what he has, that renders him truly great'; and 'that he might have an opportunity of returning his kindness.' David 'did not take Barzillai's kindness to him as a debt,' as an arbitrary prince might, but 'accepted it and rewarded it as a favour.' From this Henry draws the plain duty of gratitude: 'We must always study to be grateful to our friends, especially to those who have helped us in distress.' The commentators thus set the verse as the hinge between the two courtesies — the king pressing the fullest reward his power can offer, the old man about to decline it with a grace equal to the king's own. What David proposes is nothing less than to make his aged friend a permanent guest of the crown, sharing the king's own fare in the king's own city; and the witnesses admire in it the mark of a truly noble heart, that having received much in its need, it counts itself in debt and longs to give back manifold.
34:
Barzillai answers the king's warm invitation with a question rather than a refusal — 'how long have I to live, that I should go up with the king unto Jerusalem?' — and the witnesses read in it the modesty of a man reckoning honestly with his own last days. John Gill notes only that this is Barzillai's reply 'to the grateful proposal' David had made; the fuller weight of the answer the commentators draw from the verses it opens.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the sense in full: Barzillai asks 'how many days are there of the years of my life, that I should go up with the king to Jerusalem? I am now eighty years old,' and then, in the questions that follow, measures his fitness for a court by the failing of his senses — can he still discern good from evil, taste what he eats, or hear the voice of singers? The question of this verse is thus the opening of a meditation on mortality. Barzillai does not so much decline the honour as observe that a man so near the grave has little use for it.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, expounding the whole speech, hears in it the wisdom of age turning from the pleasures and business of the court. Barzillai 'begs his majesty's pardon for refusing so generous an offer'; he 'should think himself very happy in being near the king,' but he is old, and unfit for the diversions and the affairs of Jerusalem alike. The witnesses admire the frankness of the reckoning. Where younger men grasp at preferment, Barzillai counts his remaining days and finds a court no fit place to spend them; the nearness of death has taught him to measure every offer by its usefulness to a man about to die. The commentators read the verse as the beginning of one of Scripture's most graceful portraits of old age — clear-eyed about its own decline, grateful without grasping, ready to let the young enjoy what the old can no longer relish. Barzillai's opening question sets the key for all that follows: not ingratitude, but the settled sense of a man who knows his sun is near its setting and would rather set at home than shine borrowed light at another's table.
35:
Barzillai numbers the infirmities of his age with unflinching plainness — he cannot discern good from evil, cannot taste his food or drink, cannot hear the voice of the singers — and the witnesses take the verse both as a portrait of failing years and as a window into an ancient court. John Gill fixes the age: Barzillai was 'about such an age, very little under or over; very probably he was full that age' of fourscore years.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the catalogue of losses as 'at once an affecting description of the infirmities of old age and a correct account of the mode of living at an Eastern court in ancient times.' Barzillai's ear had 'become dull of hearing, and his relish for his food was gone'; he was 'not only an old man, but an infirm old man.' And the mention of singing men and singing women opens a window: 'vocal music constituted a principal part of court entertainments,' and since David was himself 'the sweet singer of Israel,' Clarke supposes 'he had his court well supplied with vocal as well as instrumental performers.' The delights Barzillai declines are the very delights a musical king's table would have offered in fullest measure.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley distils the same confession: 'My senses are grown dull, and incapable of relishing the pleasures of a court.
