I am past taking pleasure in delicious tastes, or sweet music, and other such delights. I am through age both useless and burdensome to others, and therefore most improper for a court life.' The witnesses find in this no complaint but a Christian realism about the body's decline. Matthew Henry gathers the lesson: as it was with Moses and with Barzillai, so it is still, that if men reach fourscore years 'their strength then is labour and sorrow,' the days of which men say they have no pleasure in them. 'Dainties are insipid when desire fails,' and 'let those that are old learn of Barzillai to be dead to the delights of sense; let grace second nature, and make a virtue of the necessity.' The commentators hold Barzillai up as a model of aging well — honest about his weakness, unwilling to be a burden, and content to leave the banquets and music of the court to those young enough to enjoy them. His inventory of losses becomes, in their reading, a quiet wisdom that knows when to withdraw.
36:
Barzillai asks only to go 'a little way over Jordan with the king,' wondering why the king should recompense so small a service with so great a reward, and the witnesses read the verse as the modesty of true worth. John Gill gives the plain sense: Barzillai will 'go a little way after he was over Jordan with the king, and then return to his own city.' The escort he offers is brief — a few steps beyond the river — and he cannot see why such a trifle should earn a place at the royal table.

The witnesses set this against the fuller portrait of Barzillai's humility that runs through the whole exchange. Matthew Henry draws it out: Barzillai 'admires the king's generosity, lessening his own service and magnifying the king's return for it: Why should the king recompense it with such a reward?' The old man reckons his kindness a small thing and the king's proposed repayment a vast one, and cannot make the two agree. Henry hears in it the astonishment that becomes every servant before an over-generous master: 'Will the master thank that servant who only does what was his duty to do? He thought he had done himself honour enough in doing the king any service.'

And here the commentators reach for the theological chord the whole scene sounds. Henry makes it explicit: 'Thus, when the saints shall be called to inherit the kingdom in consideration of what they have done for Christ in this world, they will be amazed at the disproportion between the service and the recompense' (Matthew 25:37) — 'Lord, when saw we thee hungry, and fed thee?' Barzillai's wondering question becomes a figure of the humility of the redeemed, who will stand amazed that so poor a service should be crowned with so rich a reward. The witnesses find in the aged Gileadite's self-deprecation the very temper of grace: he thinks himself honoured merely to have served, counts his little kindness no ground of claim, and marvels that the king should offer him anything at all. In a chapter thronged with men pressing their deserts, Barzillai alone protests that he has done nothing worth rewarding — and it is precisely this that marks him the worthiest of them all.
37:
Barzillai asks leave to turn back and die in his own city, by the grave of his father and mother, and to send his son Chimham to court in his place; and the witnesses read the request as the fitting close of a life that knows its own hour. John Gill gives the plain movement: Barzillai would 'turn back again to his own city, after he is gone over Jordan and seen the king a little way on his journey.'

The commentators identify Chimham and follow his fortunes. Adam Clarke holds that Chimham 'was Barzillai's son,' as appears from David's dying charge to Solomon to 'show kindness to the sons of Barzillai' (1 Kings 2:7); and he notes that long afterward mention is made of 'the habitation of Chimham, which was near to Bethlehem' (Jeremiah 41:17), reasonably conjecturing that David 'had left that portion, which was probably a part of his paternal estate, to this son of Barzillai.' Charles Ellicott reads the evidence the same way, that David 'conveyed to Chimham a house upon his own paternal estate' by Bethlehem.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes the verse a meditation on dying well. 'It is good for us all, but it especially becomes old people, to think and speak much of dying.' Barzillai turns from the offer of a court to the thought of his tomb: 'Let me go home and die in my own city, the place of my father's sepulchre; the grave is ready for me, let me go and get ready for it, go and die in my nest.' Yet his care for Chimham shows a generous heart to the last: 'those that are old must not grudge young people those delights which they themselves are past the enjoyment of.' The witnesses admire the whole disposition — a man ready for his grave, content to withdraw, yet eager that his son should have the honour he himself declines. Barzillai asks nothing for himself but a quiet death at home, and everything he might have taken he passes on to the next generation. The commentators read his 'die in my own city' as the serene wish of faith, and his commendation of Chimham as the last kindness of a man who gave freely all his life and gives still in his going.
38:
David grants Barzillai's request in full, promising to take Chimham and to do for him whatever seems good, and the witnesses read the king's answer as generosity that will not be refused. John Gill notes the substance simply: David 'admitted of him' — that is, of Chimham — 'instead of his father,' receiving the son in the place the father had declined.

The commentators set the exchange within the larger portrait of David's gratitude that Matthew Henry had drawn. Unable to keep Barzillai himself, the king transfers his kindness to the son and leaves the terms open: 'Whatsoever thou shalt think of, when thou comest home, to ask of me, that will I do for thee.' Henry hears in this the noblest use of power — 'What is the chief excellency of power but this, that it gives men a capacity of doing the more good?' David counts his kingship enriched not by what it can take but by what it can bestow, and he presses upon the old man a standing promise to grant any future request.

The witnesses find in the whole transaction a study of gratitude answered by gratitude. Barzillai had asked only a quiet death at home and a place at court for his son; David gives the second gladly and honours the first, sending the father back with his blessing and taking the son forward with a blank cheque of royal favour. Henry supposes that Barzillai, 'who had experienced the innocency and safety of retirement, begged a country seat for Chimham near Jerusalem, but not in it,' which would account for the later 'habitation of Chimham' by Bethlehem. The commentators read David here as the model of the grateful great man — one who, having received kindness in his distress, will not rest until he has repaid it, and who, when the benefactor declines reward for himself, insists on lavishing it upon his house. The verse is brief, but the witnesses weigh it as the seal of a friendship: the king's word 'I will do to him what shall seem good unto thee' leaves the reward's measure in Barzillai's own hands, a courtesy that crowns the whole affecting interview and sends the aged friend home honoured to the last.
39:
The people cross the Jordan, and at the parting the king kisses Barzillai and blesses him, and the old man turns home; the witnesses read the farewell as the tender close of the noblest friendship in the chapter. John Gill marks the plain fact that 'all the people that were with David' went over the river. Keil and Delitzsch fill in the scene: when the king had crossed, 'he kissed Barzillai' — to take leave of him, as Naomi kissed her daughters-in-law (Ruth 1:9) — 'and he, Barzillai, blessed him, and turned to his place,' that is, returned home. They note that Barzillai 'only escorted the king over the Jordan,' the whole conversation having passed as they crossed.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the two gestures as the language of parting love: 'the kiss was the token of friendship and farewell; the blessing was a prayer to God for his prosperity, probably a prophetical benediction.' The exchange is mutual and complete — the king gives the kiss of honour and affection, the subject gives the blessing of a godly old man, and each sends the other on his way enriched.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott pauses on the phrase 'all the people,' insisting on its narrowed sense: as 'Israel' has been used throughout this narrative for Absalom's supporters, so 'the people' here means 'those faithful to David.' The distinction matters, for it prepares the quarrel about to break out, in which the loyal escort of the king becomes the occasion of a fresh division. The witnesses linger over the beauty of the moment before that discord. Here, at the river's edge, is loyalty rewarded and love returned: the king who had come home over the water bids farewell to the friend who had sustained him in exile, and the two part with a kiss and a blessing, each honouring the other. The commentators read it as a scene of rest and gratitude set deliberately between the reconciliations behind and the strife ahead — a last clear note of harmony before the men of Israel and Judah fall to their bitter dispute. Barzillai turns to his own place to die in peace; David goes on to Gilgal to face a kingdom still fractured beneath its restored king.
40:
The king moves on to Gilgal, escorted by all Judah and half of Israel, and the witnesses see in that uneven attendance the spark of a new quarrel. John Gill notes the short stage from the Jordan — Gilgal, according to Josephus, 'was fifty furlongs' off, some six miles and a quarter. Keil and Delitzsch lay out the company: 'all the people of Judah had brought the king over, and also half the people of Israel' — beside Shimei's thousand Benjamites, 'other Israelites who dwelt in the neighbourhood' — and with this the historians see 'the quarrel between Israel and Judah about the restoration of the king' begin.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott explains the imbalance without blame on either side. Judah, 'deeply moved by the measures and words of David, had united generally in his restoration'; the other tribes, though they had first proposed to return to their allegiance (see 2 Samuel 19:9-10), 'had not had time to join in the present movement, or had not generally known of it,' so that only Shimei's Benjamites and those living near, with the tribes east of Jordan, could come — 'half the people of Israel' in all. John Wesley reads the contrast more pointedly: 'whereas the men of Judah came entirely and unanimously to the king, the Israelites of the other tribes came in but slowly, and by halves,' being 'no less guilty of the rebellion' yet 'not encouraged to come in by such a gracious message as they were.' This unevenness, Wesley notes, is 'the occasion both of the contention here following, and of the sedition' of the next chapter.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sets the scene of the coming strife: the Israelite elders 'had come to wait upon him, to kiss his hand and congratulate his return, but found they came too late to witness the solemnity of his first entrance,' and this 'put them out of humour,' occasioning a quarrel that 'was a damp to the joy of the day, and the beginning of further mischief.' The witnesses thus read the verse as a study in how the very act of honouring the king could divide his subjects. A restoration meant to reunite the nation instead reopens the old jealousy between Judah and Israel, and the commentators, with the narrator, mark it as the seed of Sheba's revolt to come — a sobering note that even a day of triumph carries within it the makings of the next division.
41:
The men of Israel arrive with a grievance — 'why have our brethren the men of Judah stolen thee away?' — and the witnesses trace the complaint to the deep and ancient jealousy between the tribes. Keil and Delitzsch report the scene at Gilgal: the representatives of the other tribes came to meet the king, and 'being annoyed at the fact that the men of Judah had anticipated them,' cried, 'Why have our brethren the men of Judah stolen thee away?' — fetched thee secretly, that is, 'without saying a word to us.' John Gill identifies the speakers as 'the other part that did not come over with David, some of their principal men, who met him upon the road.'

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the outburst in its long history. 'Jealousies between the tribes, and especially between Judah on the one side and the ten tribes on the other, had all along existed,' the tribe of Ephraim being 'particularly sensitive' (compare Judges 8:1; Judges 12:1). By the wars of Saul these jealousies had been held in check, but 'broke out in national separation on his death'; David partially healed them, Solomon kept them in abeyance, 'but at his death they broke out with fresh power, and dismembered the nation for ever.' The quarrel of this verse is thus a tremor of the fault line that will one day split the kingdom.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley weighs the charge. 'All' the men of Israel means 'such as were present'; to 'steal' the king was 'to convey him over Jordan hastily, not expecting our concurrence'; and it is 'mentioned as an aggravation of their fault' that Judah brought over not only the king but 'all his men too, without asking their advice.' The witnesses read the complaint as at once understandable and unjust — understandable, because the northern tribes had genuinely wished to be first (2 Samuel 19:9-10) and felt slighted at being forestalled; unjust, because the delay was their own, and Judah had only done promptly what all had resolved to do. The commentators find here the perennial mischief of wounded pride, which turns a good work done by others into a grievance against them, and they mark it as the beginning of a division that mere loyalty to a common king could not heal.
42:
Judah answers the complaint with two pleas — nearness of kin to the king, and freedom from any private gain — and the witnesses weigh both. Keil and Delitzsch render the retort: 'The king stands near to us,' inasmuch as he belonged to their tribe, 'and wherefore then art thou angry at this matter? Have we eaten from the king,' derived any advantage from the relationship, 'or received anything for ourselves therefrom?' Adam Clarke gives the sense plainly: 'We have not done this for our own advantage; we have gained nothing by it; we did it through loyal attachment to our king.'

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott draws out what lies beneath the words. Judah 'justifies its course by its nearness of relationship to the king, and repels the idea of having received any especial favours from him.' In that denial he hears a possible barb: 'in this, then, may be a taunt to the Benjamites on account of the partiality shown them by Saul' (compare 1 Samuel 22:7), where Saul had rewarded his own tribe with fields and vineyards. Against this Judah's claim to have taken nothing shines the brighter. And Ellicott finds in the whole dispute an unexpected witness to good government: 'the whole dispute is a remarkable testimony to the fairness of David's government as between the tribes,' since Judah could truthfully deny any favouritism.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the two arguments together. Judah pleads kinship — the king was 'of the same tribe with us, and therefore both oweth the more respect to us, and might expect more respect from us' — and disclaims all profit: 'we have neither sought nor gained any advantage to ourselves hereby, but only discharged our duty to the king, and used all expedition in bringing him back, which you also should have done.' The witnesses find the argument sound in substance: Judah had done the right thing promptly and for the right reason, and had taken nothing for it. Yet the commentators are already uneasy at the temper of the exchange, for a dispute conducted in the language of rival claims, however just each side's case, is a dispute that will not end well. The verse shows loyalty curdling into rivalry, each tribe pressing its title to the king rather than rejoicing in his return, and the witnesses read it as the sad spectacle of a good cause pleaded in a bad spirit.
43:
The men of Israel push their claim higher — 'we have ten parts in the king, and more right in David than ye' — and the quarrel ends with the ominous note that Judah's words were fiercer than Israel's. The witnesses parse the arithmetic first. Keil and Delitzsch explain that Israel 'considered they had ten shares in the king, because they formed ten tribes, in opposition to the one tribe of Judah'; though David was of Judah, he was king of the whole nation, so the ten tribes claimed the larger stake. John Wesley reckons the count — 'they say but ten, though strictly there were eleven' — either because Joseph, comprising Ephraim and Manasseh, was counted one, or because Simeon, whose lot lay within Judah, was joined with it.

The witnesses divide gently over whose claim was the stronger. Adam Clarke judges that the men of Judah's words 'were fiercer, for they had more weight, for they had more reason on their side'; yet he notes the greater guilt Israel could not shake — it was they who had been 'the first beginners, and the most zealous promoters of this rebellion.' John Wesley lets Israel state its double title: as the far greatest part of David's subjects they had 'more right in the king and kingdom,' and 'as he is king, we justly claim a greater interest in him than you.' Where Judah pleaded blood, Israel pleaded numbers, and each thought its own claim decisive.

The commentators fix, with the narrator, on the temper of the dispute. Judah's answer was 'fiercer,' and Matthew Henry reads the Scripture's notice of it as a rebuke: 'Though we have right and reason on our side, yet, if we express ourselves with fierceness, God takes notice of it and is much displeased.' The fiercer words 'produced very bad effects,' and 'much evil might be avoided if men would watch against pride, and remember that a soft answer turneth away wrath.' John Wesley marks the outcome — Israel was answered 'with greater fierceness, so that David durst not interpose' — and sees in it the direct occasion of Sheba's revolt in the chapter following. The chapter that opened with a king's grief thus closes with a nation's discord, and the witnesses draw the sober moral that a good cause urged in a proud and heated spirit sows the seed of strife. Adam Clarke ends on the larger reflection that the peace of a realm depends on king and people vying in mutual love rather than in rival claims — 'it is much easier to unsettle than to settle, to pull down than to build up.'
